summary: This advice column addresses three questions: how professional women should handle inappropriate personal questions from journalists about marriage and motherhood, how to navigate sexual compatibility with intellectually compatible but inexperienced partners, and how to explain abstract algebra to non-mathematicians. The gender dynamics question draws on examples of Swedish CEO Annika Falkengren and German minister Ursula von der Leyen to illustrate double standards in how media treats successful women. O'Neil argues that men often try to force women into boxes of 'sex object' or 'mother' and that deviation from these norms shouldn't require explanation or apology. The column blends serious feminist critique with practical advice delivered in a characteristically direct style.
key_points: Journalists probe women's personal lives about marriage and children in ways they never would with men in equivalent positions
Men who feel threatened by successful women try to categorize them as either sex objects or mothers, and confusion when women don't fit becomes 'your problem to explain'
Women should turn inappropriate questions back on interviewers and don't need to be polite with journalists who have 'super thick skins'
People who deviate from 'normal' should be celebrated rather than required to justify their choices
When communicating complex technical work to laypeople, connect abstract concepts to familiar structures they already understand
money_quotes: Some men are deeply threatened by women and want to put them into one of two boxes: sex object or mother.
If you are neither it makes them very confused. They want that confusion to be your problem, not theirs.
In general it's the people who deviate from normal that make life worth living. Abnormal people should be celebrated, not reprimanded.
You don't have to be polite with journalists. They have super thick skins.
It's totally fine to respond with, 'this is off the record, but your question is totally inappropriate and I won't agree to finish this interview until you agree to remove it from this interview.'
Make them aware of how inappropriate it is.
key_insight: Women in professional settings shouldn't feel obligated to justify or apologize for deviating from traditional expectations around marriage and motherhood—the burden of that discomfort belongs to those asking inappropriate questions, not those being asked.
